item.title: Minor Edit Request
item.content:encoded:
Any mail associated with SCP-3185 is to be removed from and confiscated for study.

Removed from what? I can guess at what the missing word is, but the rules as I understand them don't allow readers to edit an author's piece to insert missing words. (You could also just delete "removed from and" to make the sentence work, but again, I think that's beyond the scope of allowed reader edits.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Unless I am much mistaken, 1922 was not public domain in 2004, so you might want that giant letter to try to send the guy back a little farther.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh, I don't think I will. I believe it serves as a nice way of reminding American readers that annual public domain entry is back :)

Speaking of which, 1924 is public domain as of the 1st! :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Enjoyed it. +1

However, at this point, those from 1923 are already falling into public domain, so we might want to update it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do the letters contain more pages of information than was provided in the article? How do the subjects know they can't steal the books, or have a time limit? Is not knowing all the information part of the punishment?

I still enjoyed this skip +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you want to see some of my headcanons for the PDPS, this article now has a tale: ANTI-PUBLIC Interference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This SCP is fantastic. Project Gutenberg is fantastic. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a pet peeve of yours too? :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1. This is my favorite article on the site. Not exaggerating.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah that part largely slipped my by (pretty obvious test to do now that I think about it).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As mentioned in the containment procedures, a few control tests have been done. The subjects were briefed beforehand and amnesticized afterwords if they succeeded. As with D-Class, some were deliberately instructed to sabotage their mission, so when they didn't return it wasn't hard to infer the cause.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a fairly straightforward article but the anomaly is memorable and I like the idea of an anomalous organization punishing people involved in copyright.

Minor quibble:

If the subject fails to retrieve their book within twenty-four hours, steals or damages the book, or fails to send it to the literary foundation mentioned within forty-eight hours, they will disappear and cannot be recovered.

How does the Foundation know that this is what causes subjects to disappear? The letters don't mention this and it doesn't seem like the subjects who succeed know what will happen if they fail, so it isn't clear how the Foundation would figure this out.

Other than that, +1.

item.title: You know how 3085 was a love letter?
item.content:encoded:
This is a hate letter.

Letter was made by me. The address is Project Gutenberg's actual mailing address listed on their website, but don't send them books; instead, consider donating money or volunteering for Distributed Proofreaders.
